title: Economics and Investing Foundations
description: A practical quiz for early learners who want to understand economics, markets, investing, risk, interest rates, inflation, and asset classes beyond basic budgeting.

questions.prompt: What does GDP mainly measure in an economy?

options.label: The total market value of final goods and services produced within a country over a period
options.explanation: GDP measures the market value of final goods and services produced within a country during a specific period, so it is a broad measure of economic output.

options.label: The total amount of money held in bank accounts by households
The total profit earned by all companies listed on the stock market
The total taxes collected by the government in one year

questions.explanation: GDP measures the market value of final goods and services produced within a country during a specific period, so it is a broad measure of economic output.

questions.prompt: What does opportunity cost mean?

options.label: The fee paid to open an investment account
The value of the next best alternative you give up when making a choice
options.explanation: Opportunity cost is the value of the next best option you give up when you choose one use of money, time, or resources over another.

options.label: The total amount of money lost in a bad investment
The tax charged on investment gains

questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is the value of the next best option you give up when you choose one use of money, time, or resources over another.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between a nominal return and a real return?

options.label: Nominal return is before inflation; real return adjusts for inflation
options.explanation: A nominal return is the return stated in money terms, while a real return adjusts that result for inflation and better reflects purchasing power.

options.label: Nominal return is guaranteed; real return is always risky
Nominal return applies only to bonds; real return applies only to stocks
Nominal return is after taxes; real return is before taxes

questions.explanation: A nominal return is the return stated in money terms, while a real return adjusts that result for inflation and better reflects purchasing power.

questions.prompt: What does an interest rate generally do in the economy?

options.label: It sets the exact future price of every stock
It acts as a price of borrowing and a reward for lending or saving
options.explanation: An interest rate is often understood as the price of borrowing money and the compensation lenders or savers receive for delaying consumption and taking risk.

options.label: It guarantees that inflation will disappear
It decides which companies are allowed to sell shares

questions.explanation: An interest rate is often understood as the price of borrowing money and the compensation lenders or savers receive for delaying consumption and taking risk.

questions.prompt: If prices rise by 5% while your money grows by 3%, what happened to your purchasing power?

options.label: It increased by about 8%
It stayed exactly the same
It decreased by about 2%
options.explanation: Your purchasing power decreased because your money grew more slowly than prices. The approximate real return is 3% minus 5%, or about -2%.

options.label: It doubled because both numbers are positive

questions.explanation: Your purchasing power decreased because your money grew more slowly than prices. The approximate real return is 3% minus 5%, or about -2%.

questions.prompt: What does a recession usually describe?

options.label: A period when an economy's activity contracts significantly
options.explanation: A recession describes a significant decline in economic activity, often visible in output, employment, income, and spending.

options.label: A period when every company loses money
A period when inflation is always zero
A period when stock prices must rise quickly

questions.explanation: A recession describes a significant decline in economic activity, often visible in output, employment, income, and spending.

questions.prompt: Which price index is commonly used to track changes in the cost of a consumer basket?
questions.explanation: The Consumer Price Index, or CPI, tracks changes in prices paid by consumers for a basket of goods and services and is commonly used as an inflation measure.

questions.prompt: If the supply of a product increases while demand stays similar, what usually happens to its price?

options.label: The price usually rises because there are fewer products
The price usually falls because sellers compete to sell more available products
options.explanation: When supply increases and demand is roughly unchanged, sellers often need to compete more, which tends to put downward pressure on price.

options.label: The price must stay fixed by definition
The price becomes unrelated to supply and demand

questions.explanation: When supply increases and demand is roughly unchanged, sellers often need to compete more, which tends to put downward pressure on price.

questions.prompt: If demand for a limited product increases while supply stays similar, what is the usual price pressure?

options.label: Downward pressure
No pressure at all
Upward pressure
options.explanation: Higher demand for the same limited supply usually creates upward pressure on price because more buyers are competing for the same amount of goods or assets.

options.label: The product becomes free

questions.explanation: Higher demand for the same limited supply usually creates upward pressure on price because more buyers are competing for the same amount of goods or assets.

questions.prompt: What is one main role of a central bank?

options.label: To directly choose the best stocks for citizens
To manage monetary policy and support price stability
options.explanation: A central bank uses monetary policy tools, such as policy rates and asset operations, to influence financial conditions and support goals like price stability.

options.label: To set the salary of every worker
To guarantee profits for commercial banks

questions.explanation: A central bank uses monetary policy tools, such as policy rates and asset operations, to influence financial conditions and support goals like price stability.

questions.prompt: What is a policy interest rate?

options.label: A rate that influences borrowing costs across the financial system
options.explanation: A policy interest rate is a central bank rate that influences other interest rates in the economy, including borrowing and saving rates.

options.label: A fixed return that every stock investor receives
A tax rate charged only on luxury goods
A company's internal profit margin

questions.explanation: A policy interest rate is a central bank rate that influences other interest rates in the economy, including borrowing and saving rates.

questions.prompt: What is a bond, in simple investing terms?

options.label: A share of ownership in a company
A loan made by an investor to a borrower such as a government or company
options.explanation: A bond is essentially a loan: the investor lends money to an issuer, and the issuer promises interest payments and repayment under agreed terms.

options.label: A type of bank account with no risk
A guarantee that stock prices will not fall

questions.explanation: A bond is essentially a loan: the investor lends money to an issuer, and the issuer promises interest payments and repayment under agreed terms.

questions.prompt: What is a bond coupon?

options.label: The bond's regular interest payment
options.explanation: A coupon is the regular interest payment that a bond issuer promises to pay to bondholders.

options.label: The bond's stock-market ticker
The fee paid to sell a bond early
The tax refund attached to a bond

questions.explanation: A coupon is the regular interest payment that a bond issuer promises to pay to bondholders.

questions.prompt: What does owning a stock generally represent?

options.label: Ownership in part of a company
options.explanation: A stock represents equity ownership in a company, which means the investor participates in the company's risks and potential rewards.

options.label: A loan to a company with guaranteed repayment
A fixed monthly income contract
A government insurance policy

questions.explanation: A stock represents equity ownership in a company, which means the investor participates in the company's risks and potential rewards.

questions.prompt: What is the common term for a cash payment that a company may distribute to shareholders from profits?
questions.explanation: A dividend is a payment a company may make to shareholders, usually from profits or accumulated cash.

questions.prompt: Why can a company's stock price move even when today's profits have not changed?

options.label: Stock prices reflect expectations about the future, not only current results
options.explanation: Stock prices are forward-looking and can move when investors change expectations about future profits, risk, interest rates, or growth.

options.label: Stock prices are legally required to change every day
Only current profits matter, so this cannot happen
The central bank directly sets each stock price

questions.explanation: Stock prices are forward-looking and can move when investors change expectations about future profits, risk, interest rates, or growth.

questions.prompt: What three-letter abbreviation is commonly used for an exchange-traded fund?
questions.explanation: ETF stands for exchange-traded fund, a fund that trades on an exchange like a stock and often holds many underlying securities.

questions.prompt: What does an index fund usually try to do?

options.label: Track the performance of a specific market index
options.explanation: An index fund usually aims to track a chosen index, such as a broad stock or bond market index, instead of trying to pick individual winners.

options.label: Pick only one stock and hold it forever
Guarantee a higher return than every active fund
Avoid all market risk

questions.explanation: An index fund usually aims to track a chosen index, such as a broad stock or bond market index, instead of trying to pick individual winners.

questions.prompt: What does diversification mainly try to reduce?

options.label: The risk of depending too much on one investment or one source of return
options.explanation: Diversification spreads money across different investments so that one bad outcome has less impact on the whole portfolio.

options.label: The need to understand any investment
All possible losses in every market condition
The amount of money needed to start investing

questions.explanation: Diversification spreads money across different investments so that one bad outcome has less impact on the whole portfolio.

questions.prompt: What does liquidity mean in investing?

options.label: How easily an asset can be converted into cash without a large price discount
options.explanation: Liquidity describes how easily an asset can be bought or sold for cash without strongly affecting its price.

options.label: How quickly a company pays dividends
How much inflation exists in the economy
How often a central bank meets

questions.explanation: Liquidity describes how easily an asset can be bought or sold for cash without strongly affecting its price.

questions.prompt: What is the risk-return tradeoff?

options.label: The idea that higher expected returns usually require accepting higher risk
options.explanation: Investors usually demand higher expected returns for taking higher risk, although higher risk never guarantees higher actual returns.

options.label: The idea that risk disappears when returns are high
The rule that every risky investment must lose money
The guarantee that safe assets always outperform risky assets

questions.explanation: Investors usually demand higher expected returns for taking higher risk, although higher risk never guarantees higher actual returns.

questions.prompt: What does time horizon mean for an investor?

options.label: The length of time before the investor expects to need the money
options.explanation: Time horizon is how long an investor can keep money invested before needing it, which strongly affects suitable risk levels.

options.label: The number of stocks in a portfolio
The time of day when markets open
The exact date when the next recession will start

questions.explanation: Time horizon is how long an investor can keep money invested before needing it, which strongly affects suitable risk levels.

questions.prompt: What does compounding do to investment growth?

options.label: It lets returns earn returns over time
options.explanation: Compounding occurs when returns are reinvested and then generate additional returns, which can make time a powerful factor in growth.

options.label: It removes the possibility of loss
It converts stocks into bonds
It makes inflation irrelevant

questions.explanation: Compounding occurs when returns are reinvested and then generate additional returns, which can make time a powerful factor in growth.

questions.prompt: If an investment earns 6% and inflation is 2%, what is the approximate real return?
questions.explanation: A simple approximation for real return is nominal return minus inflation, so 6% minus 2% is about 4%.

questions.prompt: What does yield usually refer to for an investment?

options.label: The income return relative to the price or amount invested
options.explanation: Yield usually expresses income from an investment, such as interest or dividends, relative to the investment's price or value.

options.label: The number of shares a company has issued
The official inflation target of a central bank
The percentage of people unemployed

questions.explanation: Yield usually expresses income from an investment, such as interest or dividends, relative to the investment's price or value.

questions.prompt: What does market capitalization mean?

options.label: A company's total debt divided by its sales
The total market value of a company's outstanding shares
options.explanation: Market capitalization is calculated as share price multiplied by the number of shares outstanding, representing the market value of a company's equity.

options.label: The amount of cash in a country's economy
The yearly fee charged by an ETF

questions.explanation: Market capitalization is calculated as share price multiplied by the number of shares outstanding, representing the market value of a company's equity.

questions.prompt: What is the term for broad groups of investments such as stocks, bonds, cash, real estate, and commodities?
questions.explanation: Asset classes are broad categories of investments that often have different risk, return, and liquidity characteristics.

questions.prompt: What does the unemployment rate generally measure?

options.label: The share of the labor force that is without work and actively seeking work
options.explanation: The unemployment rate measures the portion of the labor force that is unemployed and actively looking for work.

options.label: The share of all citizens who own stocks
The percentage of companies that went bankrupt
The percentage change in consumer prices

questions.explanation: The unemployment rate measures the portion of the labor force that is unemployed and actively looking for work.

questions.prompt: Which statement best separates fiscal policy from monetary policy?

options.label: Fiscal policy involves government spending and taxes; monetary policy involves central bank money and interest-rate tools
options.explanation: Fiscal policy is mainly about government spending and taxation, while monetary policy is mainly conducted by central banks through money, rates, and financial conditions.

options.label: Fiscal policy is done by companies; monetary policy is done by households
Fiscal policy sets stock prices; monetary policy sets company salaries
Fiscal policy only affects bonds; monetary policy only affects real estate

questions.explanation: Fiscal policy is mainly about government spending and taxation, while monetary policy is mainly conducted by central banks through money, rates, and financial conditions.

questions.prompt: What is a capital gain?

options.label: A profit from selling an asset for more than its purchase price
options.explanation: A capital gain occurs when an asset is sold for more than its purchase price.

options.label: A regular interest payment from a bond
A fee charged by a broker
A central bank's profit target

questions.explanation: A capital gain occurs when an asset is sold for more than its purchase price.

questions.prompt: How is compound interest different from simple interest?

options.label: Compound interest earns interest on past interest; simple interest does not
options.explanation: Compound interest adds earned interest to the principal, allowing future interest to be earned on a larger base.

options.label: Simple interest always pays more than compound interest
Compound interest only applies to stocks
Simple interest adjusts for inflation automatically

questions.explanation: Compound interest adds earned interest to the principal, allowing future interest to be earned on a larger base.

questions.prompt: What is credit risk in a bond?

options.label: The risk that the bond issuer may fail to make promised payments
options.explanation: Credit risk is the possibility that the borrower issuing the bond will not make interest or principal payments as promised.

options.label: The risk that the stock market opens late
The risk that inflation is reported monthly
The risk that the bond pays interest too often

questions.explanation: Credit risk is the possibility that the borrower issuing the bond will not make interest or principal payments as promised.

questions.prompt: What does an exchange rate describe?

options.label: The price of one currency in terms of another currency
options.explanation: An exchange rate tells you how much of one currency is needed to buy another currency.

options.label: The total number of shares traded in a day
The interest rate on a mortgage only
The exact return of a stock index

questions.explanation: An exchange rate tells you how much of one currency is needed to buy another currency.

questions.prompt: What animal-market term describes a period of generally rising stock prices?
questions.explanation: A bull market describes a period in which prices, especially stock prices, are generally rising or expected to rise.

questions.prompt: Market interest rates rise after you buy a fixed-rate bond. What usually happens to the bond's market price?

options.label: It usually rises because old coupon payments become more valuable
It usually falls because newer bonds can offer higher yields
options.explanation: When market rates rise, existing fixed-rate bonds with lower coupons become less attractive, so their market prices generally fall.

options.label: It must stay exactly at the purchase price
It becomes a stock automatically

questions.explanation: When market rates rise, existing fixed-rate bonds with lower coupons become less attractive, so their market prices generally fall.

questions.prompt: What does bond duration mainly estimate?

options.label: A bond's sensitivity to changes in interest rates
options.explanation: Duration estimates how sensitive a bond's price is to interest-rate changes. Longer duration usually means greater price sensitivity.

options.label: The exact number of companies in a bond fund
The probability that a stock pays a dividend
The tax rate on bond interest

questions.explanation: Duration estimates how sensitive a bond's price is to interest-rate changes. Longer duration usually means greater price sensitivity.

questions.prompt: If inflation remains above target for a long time, what action might a central bank consider?

options.label: Lowering rates to make borrowing more attractive
Raising policy rates to cool demand and reduce inflation pressure
options.explanation: A central bank may raise policy rates to make borrowing more expensive, cool demand, and reduce inflation pressure, although outcomes are not instant or guaranteed.

options.label: Buying every listed stock to support prices
Replacing fiscal policy with company dividends

questions.explanation: A central bank may raise policy rates to make borrowing more expensive, cool demand, and reduce inflation pressure, although outcomes are not instant or guaranteed.

questions.prompt: What is the approximate term for the interest rate after adjusting for inflation?
questions.explanation: The real interest rate adjusts a nominal interest rate for inflation, giving a clearer sense of purchasing-power return.

questions.prompt: What does an inverted yield curve usually mean?

options.label: Long-term yields are below short-term yields
options.explanation: An inverted yield curve means short-term yields are higher than long-term yields. It can signal expectations of slower growth or future rate cuts, but it is not a perfect prediction tool.

options.label: Every bond has defaulted
Stock dividends are higher than bond coupons
Inflation must be exactly zero

questions.explanation: An inverted yield curve means short-term yields are higher than long-term yields. It can signal expectations of slower growth or future rate cuts, but it is not a perfect prediction tool.

questions.prompt: You invest money you may need in six months. Which principle matters most?

options.label: Short time horizons usually call for lower volatility and higher liquidity
options.explanation: Money needed soon has less time to recover from market declines, so liquidity and lower volatility are usually more important.

options.label: Short time horizons are ideal for the riskiest assets
Time horizon does not matter if expected return is high
You should ignore liquidity if inflation exists

questions.explanation: Money needed soon has less time to recover from market declines, so liquidity and lower volatility are usually more important.

questions.prompt: What does an ETF expense ratio represent?

options.label: The fund's annual operating cost as a percentage of invested assets
options.explanation: An expense ratio is the annual cost charged by a fund, expressed as a percentage of assets. Lower costs leave more of the return for the investor.

options.label: The guaranteed annual return of the ETF
The number of stocks inside the ETF
The tax rate on ETF dividends only

questions.explanation: An expense ratio is the annual cost charged by a fund, expressed as a percentage of assets. Lower costs leave more of the return for the investor.

questions.prompt: What is a key difference between active and passive investing?

options.label: Active investing tries to outperform a benchmark; passive investing usually tries to track one
options.explanation: Active investing attempts to beat a benchmark through selection or timing, while passive investing usually aims to track a benchmark at lower cost.

options.label: Passive investing means holding only cash
Active investing is always cheaper than passive investing
Passive investing guarantees no losses

questions.explanation: Active investing attempts to beat a benchmark through selection or timing, while passive investing usually aims to track a benchmark at lower cost.

questions.prompt: What does portfolio rebalancing do?

options.label: It brings a portfolio back toward its target asset allocation
options.explanation: Rebalancing adjusts portfolio weights back toward a chosen plan, which helps manage risk after some assets rise or fall more than others.

options.label: It guarantees a positive return each year
It removes all tax obligations
It changes every investment into cash

questions.explanation: Rebalancing adjusts portfolio weights back toward a chosen plan, which helps manage risk after some assets rise or fall more than others.

questions.prompt: What is concentration risk?

options.label: The risk of having too much exposure to one company, sector, country, or asset
options.explanation: Concentration risk appears when too much of a portfolio depends on one source of performance, making the portfolio more vulnerable to a specific negative event.

options.label: The risk that a portfolio owns too many different investments
The risk that inflation reports are delayed
The risk that cash earns interest

questions.explanation: Concentration risk appears when too much of a portfolio depends on one source of performance, making the portfolio more vulnerable to a specific negative event.

questions.prompt: Why are dividends not automatically 'free money' for investors?

options.label: A company's value may fall by roughly the dividend amount when cash leaves the company
options.explanation: A dividend transfers cash from the company to shareholders, so the company's value may adjust downward by roughly the amount paid, all else equal.

options.label: Dividends are illegal in most stock markets
Dividends always mean a company is losing money
Receiving a dividend cancels stock ownership

questions.explanation: A dividend transfers cash from the company to shareholders, so the company's value may adjust downward by roughly the amount paid, all else equal.

questions.prompt: What does the price-to-earnings ratio compare?

options.label: A company's stock price to its earnings per share
options.explanation: The P/E ratio compares a company's share price with its earnings per share, giving a rough measure of how much investors pay for each unit of earnings.

options.label: A bond coupon to a central bank rate
A country's exports to its imports
A fund's expense ratio to its number of holdings

questions.explanation: The P/E ratio compares a company's share price with its earnings per share, giving a rough measure of how much investors pay for each unit of earnings.

questions.prompt: A company's earnings grow, but its stock price falls. Which explanation is most reasonable?

options.label: Investors may have expected even stronger earnings or now see higher risk
options.explanation: Stock prices depend on expectations. If results are good but worse than expected, or if risk rises, the price can still fall.

options.label: This is impossible because earnings and stock prices must always move together
The company must have stopped existing
All dividends must have been cancelled by law

questions.explanation: Stock prices depend on expectations. If results are good but worse than expected, or if risk rises, the price can still fall.

questions.prompt: What does it mean when an asset is described as overvalued?

options.label: Its market price may be high relative to its fundamentals or expected cash flows
options.explanation: An overvalued asset is priced high compared with what its fundamentals, cash flows, or risks may justify.

options.label: Its price is low enough to remove all risk
It pays too much interest by law
It cannot be traded in a market

questions.explanation: An overvalued asset is priced high compared with what its fundamentals, cash flows, or risks may justify.

questions.prompt: What does dollar-cost averaging mainly do?

options.label: It spreads purchases over time instead of investing all at once
options.explanation: Dollar-cost averaging spreads investment purchases across different dates, which can reduce the risk of investing all money just before a price decline.

options.label: It guarantees buying only at the lowest price
It removes market risk completely
It turns every investment into a bond

questions.explanation: Dollar-cost averaging spreads investment purchases across different dates, which can reduce the risk of investing all money just before a price decline.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between risk tolerance and risk capacity?

options.label: Risk tolerance is emotional comfort with risk; risk capacity is financial ability to take risk
options.explanation: Risk tolerance is how comfortable someone feels with uncertainty, while risk capacity is how much risk their finances and time horizon can actually support.

options.label: Risk tolerance applies only to bonds; risk capacity applies only to stocks
They mean exactly the same thing
Risk capacity is always higher than risk tolerance

questions.explanation: Risk tolerance is how comfortable someone feels with uncertainty, while risk capacity is how much risk their finances and time horizon can actually support.

questions.prompt: Why is volatility not exactly the same as permanent loss?

options.label: Volatility is price movement; permanent loss happens when value is destroyed or an asset is sold after an unrecovered decline
options.explanation: Volatility describes fluctuations in price. A permanent loss is different because capital is not recovered, often due to business failure, default, or selling after a major decline.

options.label: Volatility means a company has no revenue
Permanent loss happens every time a price moves
Volatility only exists in savings accounts

questions.explanation: Volatility describes fluctuations in price. A permanent loss is different because capital is not recovered, often due to business failure, default, or selling after a major decline.

questions.prompt: Which pair correctly names two major bond risks?

options.label: Default risk and interest-rate risk
options.explanation: Bond investors commonly face default risk, where the issuer may not pay, and interest-rate risk, where changing rates affect bond prices.

options.label: Dividend risk and voting risk
Inventory risk and advertising risk
Import risk and payroll risk

questions.explanation: Bond investors commonly face default risk, where the issuer may not pay, and interest-rate risk, where changing rates affect bond prices.

questions.prompt: Compared with government bonds from financially strong countries, corporate bonds usually offer higher yields mainly because they often carry more:

options.label: Credit risk
options.explanation: Corporate bonds often offer higher yields because investors require compensation for higher issuer-specific credit risk.

options.label: Voting power
Ownership rights
Central bank control

questions.explanation: Corporate bonds often offer higher yields because investors require compensation for higher issuer-specific credit risk.

questions.prompt: How does real GDP differ from nominal GDP?

options.label: Real GDP adjusts for inflation; nominal GDP uses current prices
options.explanation: Real GDP adjusts output for price changes, making it better for comparing production across time.

options.label: Real GDP counts only exports; nominal GDP counts only imports
Real GDP measures stock prices; nominal GDP measures bond prices
Real GDP is always larger than nominal GDP

questions.explanation: Real GDP adjusts output for price changes, making it better for comparing production across time.

questions.prompt: Which example is closest to a negative supply shock?

options.label: A sudden shortage of an important input raises production costs
options.explanation: A negative supply shock reduces supply or raises production costs, which can push prices higher and output lower.

options.label: Consumers become more interested in a product because of a trend
A company lowers prices after improving productivity
A central bank publishes an inflation report

questions.explanation: A negative supply shock reduces supply or raises production costs, which can push prices higher and output lower.

questions.prompt: What does price elasticity of demand describe?

options.label: How sensitive quantity demanded is to a change in price
options.explanation: Price elasticity of demand measures how much demand changes when price changes.

options.label: How quickly a company can issue new shares
How much tax a bond investor pays
How long a recession lasts

questions.explanation: Price elasticity of demand measures how much demand changes when price changes.

questions.prompt: Why do central bank rate changes affect the economy with a delay?

options.label: Borrowing, spending, hiring, and investment decisions take time to adjust
options.explanation: Monetary policy works through financial conditions and real-world decisions, and those channels usually take time to influence inflation and growth.

options.label: The rate change is secret for several years
Only stock traders can see the rate change
Prices are legally fixed after every meeting

questions.explanation: Monetary policy works through financial conditions and real-world decisions, and those channels usually take time to influence inflation and growth.

questions.prompt: Why is central bank credibility important?

options.label: People's inflation expectations can influence wage setting, pricing, and long-term interest rates
options.explanation: If people trust that a central bank will control inflation, expectations may stay better anchored, which can make inflation easier to manage.

options.label: It allows the central bank to choose every company's CEO
It guarantees that no recession can occur
It makes all government debt disappear

questions.explanation: If people trust that a central bank will control inflation, expectations may stay better anchored, which can make inflation easier to manage.

questions.prompt: What is a fiscal deficit?

options.label: When government spending exceeds government revenue over a period
options.explanation: A fiscal deficit occurs when a government spends more than it collects in revenue during a period, usually requiring borrowing.

options.label: When exports exceed imports
When a company pays a dividend
When a central bank lowers interest rates

questions.explanation: A fiscal deficit occurs when a government spends more than it collects in revenue during a period, usually requiring borrowing.

questions.prompt: Why should an investor not assume stocks and bonds always move in opposite directions?

options.label: Correlations can change depending on inflation, growth, and interest-rate conditions
options.explanation: Stock-bond correlations are not fixed. In some environments, especially when inflation or rates dominate markets, both can fall together.

options.label: Bonds are not traded in markets
Stocks and bonds are the same asset class
Diversification is illegal during inflation

questions.explanation: Stock-bond correlations are not fixed. In some environments, especially when inflation or rates dominate markets, both can fall together.

questions.prompt: What extra risk can international investing add?

options.label: Currency risk from exchange-rate movements
options.explanation: International investments can add currency risk because returns in the investor's home currency depend partly on exchange-rate movements.

options.label: The guarantee of lower returns
The removal of all diversification benefits
The obligation to buy only government bonds

questions.explanation: International investments can add currency risk because returns in the investor's home currency depend partly on exchange-rate movements.

questions.prompt: What do inflation-protected bonds try to do?

options.label: Adjust payments or principal in a way that helps protect against inflation
options.explanation: Inflation-protected bonds are designed so that payments or principal adjust with inflation, helping preserve purchasing power relative to ordinary nominal bonds.

options.label: Guarantee high stock-market returns
Remove all default risk from every issuer
Set the official inflation rate

questions.explanation: Inflation-protected bonds are designed so that payments or principal adjust with inflation, helping preserve purchasing power relative to ordinary nominal bonds.

questions.prompt: What is the common term for an agency's assessment of a borrower's ability to repay debt?
questions.explanation: A credit rating is an assessment of creditworthiness, often used by bond investors to judge default risk.

questions.prompt: What is a mutual fund's NAV?

options.label: The per-share value of the fund's assets minus liabilities
options.explanation: NAV, or net asset value, is the value of a fund's assets minus liabilities divided by the number of fund shares or units.

options.label: The fund manager's salary
The number of trades made each day
The guaranteed return of the fund

questions.explanation: NAV, or net asset value, is the value of a fund's assets minus liabilities divided by the number of fund shares or units.

questions.prompt: What is the bid-ask spread?

options.label: The difference between the price buyers offer and the price sellers ask
options.explanation: The bid-ask spread is the gap between the highest price buyers are currently willing to pay and the lowest price sellers are willing to accept.

options.label: The difference between inflation and GDP growth
The gap between a company's revenue and profit
The annual fee charged by a bond issuer

questions.explanation: The bid-ask spread is the gap between the highest price buyers are currently willing to pay and the lowest price sellers are willing to accept.

questions.prompt: Why might a limit order be safer than a market order in a thinly traded asset?

options.label: It sets a maximum buying price or minimum selling price instead of accepting any available price
options.explanation: A limit order controls the worst acceptable price, which can protect against poor execution when bid-ask spreads are wide or liquidity is low.

options.label: It guarantees the order will be filled immediately
It prevents all investment losses
It makes the asset more diversified

questions.explanation: A limit order controls the worst acceptable price, which can protect against poor execution when bid-ask spreads are wide or liquidity is low.

questions.prompt: What does reinvesting dividends do over time?

options.label: It can increase compounding by buying more shares instead of taking the cash out
options.explanation: Reinvesting dividends keeps more money working in the investment and can strengthen compounding over long periods.

options.label: It makes dividends tax-free in every country
It prevents stock prices from falling
It changes the stock into a bond

questions.explanation: Reinvesting dividends keeps more money working in the investment and can strengthen compounding over long periods.

questions.prompt: A bond fund has a duration of about 7 years. What does that roughly imply?

options.label: A 1 percentage point rise in rates could reduce its price by about 7%, before other effects
options.explanation: Duration gives a rough estimate of interest-rate sensitivity. A duration of 7 suggests a 1 percentage point rate increase may lower price by about 7%, before convexity and other factors.

options.label: The fund must mature exactly in 7 calendar years
The fund is risk-free for 7 years
The fund pays a 7% coupon by definition

questions.explanation: Duration gives a rough estimate of interest-rate sensitivity. A duration of 7 suggests a 1 percentage point rate increase may lower price by about 7%, before convexity and other factors.

questions.prompt: What does bond convexity add to duration analysis?

options.label: It accounts for the fact that the price-rate relationship is curved, not perfectly linear
options.explanation: Convexity improves duration analysis by recognizing that bond prices do not change in a perfectly straight line as yields change.

options.label: It measures only the issuer's credit rating
It guarantees that long bonds cannot lose money
It removes the need to estimate interest-rate risk

questions.explanation: Convexity improves duration analysis by recognizing that bond prices do not change in a perfectly straight line as yields change.

questions.prompt: What is the term for extra yield investors may demand for holding longer-term bonds instead of rolling short-term bonds?
questions.explanation: The term premium is the extra compensation investors may require for the risks and uncertainty of holding longer-term bonds.

questions.prompt: What does a positive real yield mean?

options.label: The yield is above expected inflation before considering taxes and other costs
options.explanation: A positive real yield means the yield is above expected inflation, so it offers a positive return in purchasing-power terms before taxes and other costs.

options.label: The investment has no risk
The nominal yield is below inflation
The bond must beat every stock index

questions.explanation: A positive real yield means the yield is above expected inflation, so it offers a positive return in purchasing-power terms before taxes and other costs.

questions.prompt: What does a discounted cash flow model try to do?

options.label: Estimate value by translating expected future cash flows into today's money
options.explanation: A discounted cash flow model estimates present value by discounting expected future cash flows back to today using a required return or discount rate.

options.label: Predict tomorrow's exact stock price
Measure only a company's social media activity
Calculate a bond's credit rating

questions.explanation: A discounted cash flow model estimates present value by discounting expected future cash flows back to today using a required return or discount rate.

questions.prompt: In valuation, what does a higher discount rate usually do to the present value of future cash flows?

options.label: It lowers present value
options.explanation: A higher discount rate reduces the value today of cash flows expected in the future because the required return is higher.

options.label: It raises present value
It has no effect
It guarantees the cash flows will occur

questions.explanation: A higher discount rate reduces the value today of cash flows expected in the future because the required return is higher.

questions.prompt: What is the common term for the extra return investors expect from stocks over safer assets?
questions.explanation: The equity risk premium is the expected extra return investors require for taking equity risk instead of holding safer assets.

questions.prompt: What does a margin of safety try to provide?

options.label: A buffer between estimated value and purchase price in case the analysis is wrong
options.explanation: A margin of safety means buying at a price sufficiently below estimated value to allow room for uncertainty and analytical errors.

options.label: A guarantee that the investment cannot fall
A way to avoid reading financial statements
A rule that only applies to bank deposits

questions.explanation: A margin of safety means buying at a price sufficiently below estimated value to allow room for uncertainty and analytical errors.

questions.prompt: What is sequence-of-returns risk?

options.label: The risk that poor returns occur at a bad time, especially when withdrawing money
options.explanation: Sequence-of-returns risk is about timing: the same average return can produce different outcomes depending on when gains and losses happen, especially during withdrawals.

options.label: The risk that all assets have the same return every year
The guarantee that early losses are always recovered immediately
The risk that dividends are paid in the wrong month

questions.explanation: Sequence-of-returns risk is about timing: the same average return can produce different outcomes depending on when gains and losses happen, especially during withdrawals.

questions.prompt: What does a high valuation ratio often imply for future expected returns, all else equal?

options.label: Future expected returns may be lower because more optimism is already priced in
options.explanation: High valuation can mean investors are paying a lot for expected future cash flows, which can reduce future expected returns if fundamentals do not exceed expectations.

options.label: Future returns are guaranteed to be higher
The company has no business risk
The asset cannot become more expensive

questions.explanation: High valuation can mean investors are paying a lot for expected future cash flows, which can reduce future expected returns if fundamentals do not exceed expectations.

questions.prompt: What term measures how sensitive a stock or portfolio is to movements in the overall market?
questions.explanation: Beta measures an investment's sensitivity to broad market movements. A beta above 1 usually means higher market sensitivity than the benchmark.

questions.prompt: What term describes return above a benchmark after adjusting for market exposure or risk model assumptions?
questions.explanation: Alpha is often used to describe performance above what a benchmark or risk model would predict, though it is difficult to achieve consistently.

questions.prompt: What is tracking error?

options.label: How much a fund's returns deviate from the returns of its benchmark
options.explanation: Tracking error measures how closely a fund follows its benchmark. Lower tracking error means performance is closer to the benchmark.

options.label: The fee paid to buy a fund
The number of stocks in an index
The tax rate applied to dividends

questions.explanation: Tracking error measures how closely a fund follows its benchmark. Lower tracking error means performance is closer to the benchmark.

questions.prompt: What is factor investing?

options.label: Investing based on characteristics such as value, size, quality, momentum, or low volatility
options.explanation: Factor investing targets broad characteristics that research links to differences in risk or return, such as value, size, momentum, quality, or low volatility.

options.label: Investing only in companies with one founder
Buying stocks randomly without any method
Holding only cash when interest rates change

questions.explanation: Factor investing targets broad characteristics that research links to differences in risk or return, such as value, size, momentum, quality, or low volatility.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes value versus growth investing?

options.label: Value focuses on lower prices relative to fundamentals; growth focuses on companies expected to grow faster
options.explanation: Value investing emphasizes price relative to fundamentals, while growth investing emphasizes companies expected to increase revenue or earnings faster.

options.label: Value means buying only bonds; growth means buying only commodities
Value stocks cannot fall; growth stocks cannot pay dividends
Both terms mean exactly the same strategy

questions.explanation: Value investing emphasizes price relative to fundamentals, while growth investing emphasizes companies expected to increase revenue or earnings faster.

questions.prompt: Why is a historical small-cap premium not a guaranteed future return?

options.label: Past patterns can weaken, disappear, or come with long periods of underperformance
options.explanation: A historical premium is not a guarantee. It may reflect risk, data history, changing market structure, or investor behavior, and it can underperform for long periods.

options.label: Small companies are legally required to outperform
Small-cap stocks have no business risk
The premium is paid by the central bank

questions.explanation: A historical premium is not a guarantee. It may reflect risk, data history, changing market structure, or investor behavior, and it can underperform for long periods.

questions.prompt: What does the efficient market hypothesis generally suggest?

options.label: Market prices quickly incorporate available information, making consistent mispricing hard to exploit
options.explanation: The efficient market hypothesis suggests that available information is reflected in prices quickly, so consistently beating the market is difficult.

options.label: Markets are always perfectly calm
No investor can ever lose money
All assets always have the same expected return

questions.explanation: The efficient market hypothesis suggests that available information is reflected in prices quickly, so consistently beating the market is difficult.

questions.prompt: What is a liquidity premium?

options.label: Extra expected return investors may demand for holding assets that are harder to sell
options.explanation: A liquidity premium compensates investors for holding assets that may be difficult, slow, or costly to sell.

options.label: A fee paid for using a checking account
A guaranteed payment from every ETF
A tax deduction for long-term investors

questions.explanation: A liquidity premium compensates investors for holding assets that may be difficult, slow, or costly to sell.

questions.prompt: What is loss aversion?

options.label: The tendency to feel losses more strongly than equivalent gains
options.explanation: Loss aversion is the tendency for people to experience the pain of losses more strongly than the pleasure of similar-sized gains.

options.label: The guarantee that losses are always larger than gains
A rule that makes bonds risk-free
The belief that prices cannot fall twice

questions.explanation: Loss aversion is the tendency for people to experience the pain of losses more strongly than the pleasure of similar-sized gains.

questions.prompt: What is confirmation bias in investing?

options.label: Looking for information that supports your existing view while ignoring contrary evidence
options.explanation: Confirmation bias can make investors overvalue information that supports their opinion and undervalue evidence that challenges it.

options.label: Selling every investment after one good year
Choosing assets only by alphabetical order
Calculating inflation incorrectly

questions.explanation: Confirmation bias can make investors overvalue information that supports their opinion and undervalue evidence that challenges it.

questions.prompt: What is recency bias?

options.label: Giving too much weight to recent events when judging the future
options.explanation: Recency bias occurs when recent performance or news feels more important than it should, leading investors to extrapolate too much from the near past.

options.label: Ignoring the most recent price completely
Believing old data is always more accurate
Choosing investments only once per year

questions.explanation: Recency bias occurs when recent performance or news feels more important than it should, leading investors to extrapolate too much from the near past.

questions.prompt: What is anchoring in financial decision-making?

options.label: Relying too heavily on an initial number or reference point
options.explanation: Anchoring happens when an initial number, such as a past price, strongly influences judgment even when new information matters more.

options.label: Changing your opinion after every new fact
Investing only in shipping companies
Using inflation data to calculate real return

questions.explanation: Anchoring happens when an initial number, such as a past price, strongly influences judgment even when new information matters more.

questions.prompt: What is herd behavior in markets?

options.label: Following the crowd mainly because others are buying or selling
options.explanation: Herd behavior occurs when investors follow others' actions rather than making independent judgments based on risk, value, and goals.

options.label: Diversifying across unrelated assets
Calculating the expected return of a bond
Rebalancing to a target allocation

questions.explanation: Herd behavior occurs when investors follow others' actions rather than making independent judgments based on risk, value, and goals.

questions.prompt: What ratio compares excess return to volatility and is often used as a risk-adjusted performance measure?
questions.explanation: The Sharpe ratio compares excess return with volatility, helping evaluate how much return was earned per unit of risk.

questions.prompt: Why is standard deviation an incomplete measure of investment risk?

options.label: It measures volatility but may not capture valuation risk, liquidity risk, behavior risk, or permanent loss
options.explanation: Standard deviation captures how much returns fluctuate, but investors also face risks such as overvaluation, illiquidity, poor behavior, default, and permanent capital loss.

options.label: It only works for banknotes
It guarantees that returns are normally distributed
It measures taxes instead of price movement

questions.explanation: Standard deviation captures how much returns fluctuate, but investors also face risks such as overvaluation, illiquidity, poor behavior, default, and permanent capital loss.

questions.prompt: A long-term nominal bond pays a fixed coupon while unexpected inflation rises sharply. What risk becomes more important?

options.label: Purchasing-power risk because fixed payments buy less in real terms
options.explanation: Unexpected inflation hurts fixed nominal payments because those payments buy fewer goods and services in real terms.

options.label: Voting risk because bondholders control the company
Dividend risk because coupons become dividends
Liquidity disappears automatically

questions.explanation: Unexpected inflation hurts fixed nominal payments because those payments buy fewer goods and services in real terms.

questions.prompt: What is reinvestment risk for a bond investor?

options.label: The risk that future coupon payments must be reinvested at lower rates
options.explanation: Reinvestment risk is the possibility that cash flows from a bond, such as coupons, will be reinvested at lower rates than expected.

options.label: The risk that the issuer pays too much interest
The risk that shares cannot be voted
The risk that an ETF has too many holdings

questions.explanation: Reinvestment risk is the possibility that cash flows from a bond, such as coupons, will be reinvested at lower rates than expected.

questions.prompt: What is a callable bond?

options.label: A bond the issuer can repay early under specified conditions
options.explanation: A callable bond gives the issuer the right to repay the bond early, often when rates fall, which can limit the investor's upside.

options.label: A bond that can only be bought by phone
A bond that has no maturity date and no coupon
A bond that automatically becomes a stock index fund

questions.explanation: A callable bond gives the issuer the right to repay the bond early, often when rates fall, which can limit the investor's upside.

questions.prompt: What does quantitative tightening generally do?

options.label: It reduces or stops the central bank's balance-sheet support by letting assets run off or selling them
options.explanation: Quantitative tightening reduces central bank balance-sheet support, often by allowing bonds to mature without full reinvestment or by selling assets.

options.label: It guarantees cheaper mortgages immediately
It means the government cuts taxes
It forces companies to issue more dividends

questions.explanation: Quantitative tightening reduces central bank balance-sheet support, often by allowing bonds to mature without full reinvestment or by selling assets.

questions.prompt: Why is the link between money supply growth and inflation not always one-for-one in the short run?

options.label: Velocity, output, expectations, banking behavior, and demand for money can change
options.explanation: Money supply matters, but the short-run inflation outcome also depends on how fast money circulates, real output, credit creation, expectations, and money demand.

options.label: Money supply has no relationship with inflation under any condition
Inflation is set only by stock-market returns
Central banks do not affect money or credit conditions

questions.explanation: Money supply matters, but the short-run inflation outcome also depends on how fast money circulates, real output, credit creation, expectations, and money demand.

questions.prompt: Why can a yield curve inversion be interpreted as a warning sign but not a certainty?

options.label: It can reflect expectations of slower growth or future rate cuts, but many other factors affect yields
options.explanation: An inversion can signal market expectations of slower growth or future rate cuts, but term premia, central bank policy, inflation expectations, and global demand also influence yields.

options.label: It means every recession has already started
It means stock returns must be negative for exactly one year
It is created only by corporate dividends

questions.explanation: An inversion can signal market expectations of slower growth or future rate cuts, but term premia, central bank policy, inflation expectations, and global demand also influence yields.

questions.prompt: What is the term for the difference between a risky bond yield and a comparable safer government bond yield?
questions.explanation: A credit spread is the extra yield investors demand for taking credit risk compared with a safer benchmark bond.

questions.prompt: Two funds track the same index. Fund A has a lower expense ratio and similar tracking quality. What is usually the better long-term feature?

options.label: The lower cost, because fees compound against the investor over time
options.explanation: When funds track the same index with similar quality, lower ongoing costs are usually beneficial because fees reduce compounded returns.

options.label: The higher cost, because fees guarantee better returns
The fund with more complicated marketing language
The fund with the highest share price

questions.explanation: When funds track the same index with similar quality, lower ongoing costs are usually beneficial because fees reduce compounded returns.
